title: Hurricane Neddy

jokes.text: Yes, but the records only go back to 1978... when the hall of records was mysteriously blown away.
jokes.setup: Homer says there's no record of hurricanes hitting Springfield
jokes.punchline: The records were destroyed by the very thing they would have documented
jokes.explanation: Brilliant circular logic joke with perfect setup/payoff structure. Classic Simpsons institutional incompetence humor.

jokes.text: What is it, boy? Fire? Earthquake? Hippies?
jokes.punchline: Hippies as equal threat to natural disasters
jokes.explanation: Great escalation with Homer's paranoid worldview. Economy of three words creates the absurd equivalency.

jokes.text: And if you think naming a destructive storm after a woman is sexist... you obviously have never seen the gals grabbing for items at a clearance sale.
jokes.punchline: Doubling down with shopping stereotype
jokes.explanation: Dated sexist humor that relies on tired stereotypes. The meta-acknowledgment doesn't elevate the lazy punchline.

jokes.text: That's true, but he shouldn't say it.
jokes.punchline: Marge's conflicted acknowledgment of truth vs propriety
jokes.explanation: Perfect Marge character beat - torn between honesty and social propriety. Elevates the previous weak joke.

jokes.text: Let's just beat him up and take his stuff! / No, no, no! Do not listen to that man. Remain calm. You will all have a chance to be gouged.
jokes.punchline: Store clerk treating price gouging as the civilized alternative
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about capitalism vs violence. Good contrast between chaos and organized exploitation.

jokes.text: Oh, there's so little left. 'Creamed Eels'? 'Corn Nog'? 'Wadded Beef.'
jokes.punchline: Increasingly disgusting fake product names
jokes.explanation: Classic Simpsons fake products. 'Wadded Beef' is particularly inspired - sounds both processed and violent.

jokes.text: But I'm not fruit! I'm a kid! / That's what the pumpkin said.
jokes.setup: Bart protesting being mistaken for produce
jokes.punchline: Apu's matter-of-fact reference to pumpkin's similar protest
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist logic delivered with perfect deadpan. The implication that vegetables regularly protest being harvested is wonderfully surreal.

jokes.text: Hi, Lisa. We're going to be in a pie!
jokes.punchline: Cheerful acceptance of gruesome fate
jokes.explanation: Great callback that extends the absurd vegetable consciousness concept. Dark humor in cheerful death acceptance.

jokes.text: I ain't leaving! I was born in this nursing home... and I'll die in this nursing home! / Is there any chance of you changing your mind? / Sure. Let's go.
jokes.punchline: Instant capitulation when politely asked
jokes.explanation: Perfect misdirection structure. The dramatic buildup makes the instant reversal more effective.

jokes.text: Oh, it'll be fun. We're gonna go through our old canceled checks... and receipts and give ourselves an audit. Make sure we don't owe anything extra.
jokes.setup: Inviting Simpsons to weather the storm
jokes.punchline: Ned's idea of fun is voluntary tax auditing
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ned Flanders characterization - his genuine enthusiasm for bureaucratic tedium is both endearing and absurd.

jokes.text: I'm sure I'd be a third wheel. / I would make it my business to be a third wheel.
jokes.punchline: Homer's honest admission of intentional antisocial behavior
jokes.explanation: Great Homer character moment - his complete honesty about his antisocial intentions. The repetition creates escalation.

jokes.text: If baby Jesus got loose, he could really do some damage.
jokes.setup: Taking down Christmas decorations for safety
jokes.punchline: Treating baby Jesus as dangerous projectile
jokes.explanation: Brilliant juxtaposition of sacred imagery with mundane safety concerns. Ned's matter-of-fact delivery makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Awesome! So long, suckers!
jokes.punchline: Bart's excitement about potential destruction
jokes.explanation: Pure Bart Simpson reaction - finding joy in chaos and destruction. Simple but character-appropriate.

jokes.text: Spin the middle side topwise. Topwise! / My main finger?
jokes.setup: Solving Rubik's Cube as group activity
jokes.punchline: Homer's made-up directional terms and anatomy
jokes.explanation: Homer's confident delivery of nonsensical instructions. 'Topwise' and 'main finger' are inspired made-up terms.

jokes.text: Spin the middle side topwise. Topwise!
jokes.punchline: Insistent repetition of made-up direction
jokes.explanation: Homer's authoritative gibberish. The repetition adds emphasis but it's a simple gag.

jokes.text: Now I remember why I put this down here in the first place!
jokes.punchline: Revelation that this exact scenario happened before
jokes.explanation: Relatable moment of remembering why you abandoned something frustrating. Good character insight into Homer's patterns.

jokes.text: I don't remember a bowling alley being there- [Screams]
jokes.punchline: Immediate contradiction as second half of hurricane hits
jokes.explanation: Perfect timing gag - the pause mid-sentence before chaos resumes creates great comedic rhythm.

jokes.text: He fell for it! Way to go, Marge!
jokes.setup: Marge's sincere prayer apparently stopping hurricane
jokes.punchline: Homer treating divine intervention as a con game
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Homer moment - his complete misunderstanding of spirituality treated as grifting. Perfect character logic.

jokes.text: It just goes to show you that everything will work out if you have faith.
jokes.punchline: Cut to Ned's completely destroyed house
jokes.explanation: Perfect ironic juxtaposition - Marge's faith vindicated immediately undercut by faithful Ned's total destruction.

jokes.text: It's all gone. Everything... gone-diddilly-on.
jokes.punchline: Ned-ism even in his darkest moment
jokes.explanation: Character consistency even in tragedy - Ned can't help his speech patterns even when devastated. Pathetic and funny.

jokes.text: I thought I was headed for the eternal bliss of paradise. / Oh, my gosh! Look at Rod! / I have a headache!
jokes.punchline: Child's mundane complaint treated as equally shocking
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection - setting up spiritual revelation then deflating to child's minor complaint. Shows Flanders family priorities.

jokes.text: Oh, well, no. Neddy doesn't believe in insurance. He considers it a form of gambling.
jokes.setup: Suggestion that insurance will cover damage
jokes.punchline: Ned's moral objection backfiring catastrophically
jokes.explanation: Perfect character-driven irony - Ned's extreme moral positions creating practical disasters. Insurance as gambling is genuinely Ned logic.

jokes.text: The only thing that survived the storm were the family tombstones. They're all we have left.
jokes.setup: Looking for silver lining in destruction
jokes.punchline: Only death-related items survived
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about the irony of death monuments being the only survivors. Macabre but fitting observation.

jokes.text: Look, Daddy. Todd is stupid, and I'm with him. / And now Mommy's stupid! [Laughs] Looking good, Rod. Looking good.
jokes.punchline: Child's innocent cruelty about family's reduced status
jokes.explanation: Child's oblivious cruelty mixed with Ned's forced positivity. Good cringe comedy from family's reduced circumstances.

jokes.text: Surprisingly, people are grabbing things with both hands... suggesting it's not just southpaws in this rampaging mob.
jokes.punchline: Observation that right-handed people are stealing left-handed items
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay connecting 'southpaws' (left-handed) to the left-handed store concept. Good observational detail.

jokes.text: Springfield bowlers will be happy to hear that the Bowlarama is back in business at its new location... teetering over the Carter Dixon Tunnel.
jokes.punchline: Completely unsafe new location
jokes.explanation: Good visual absurdity - treating extremely dangerous situation as minor inconvenience. Classic Springfield dysfunction.

jokes.text: Also, I believe Job was right-handed.
jokes.setup: Ned comparing himself to biblical Job
jokes.punchline: Lovejoy's completely irrelevant theological correction
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lovejoy character moment - missing the point entirely while being technically pedantic. Great deflation of Ned's spiritual crisis.

jokes.text: Short answer- yes, with an 'if.' Long answer- no, with a 'but.'
jokes.punchline: Deliberately confusing non-answer
jokes.explanation: Brilliant parody of evasive bureaucratic language. The structure is perfect - appears helpful while being completely useless.

jokes.text: I've got a copy of something or other by Art Linkletter in my office.
jokes.punchline: Vague recommendation of random self-help book
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Lovejoy's disconnection from his pastoral duties. The vague 'something or other' adds to the dismissiveness.

jokes.text: Son of a gun-diddilly-un! Those gilded edges smart.
jokes.setup: Ned reaches for Bible for comfort
jokes.punchline: Gets paper cut and modified swearing
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ned moment - even his profanity is sanitized. Physical comedy of being hurt by the thing meant to comfort him.

jokes.text: I've even kept kosher just to be on the safe side.
jokes.punchline: Hedging his bets with multiple religions
jokes.explanation: Great insight into Ned's anxious perfectionism - following multiple religious traditions to cover all bases.

jokes.text: I want to yell out, but I just can't... dang-diddily-do-dang, do-dang-diddily-darn do it!
jokes.punchline: Even his attempt at profanity is sanitized gibberish
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation of Ned's speech pattern - the breakdown into pure nonsense syllables shows his complete inability to swear.

jokes.text: Oh, what happened now? Did the rubble burn down?
jokes.punchline: Expecting even worse disaster
jokes.explanation: Great pessimistic escalation - imagining disaster beyond disaster. The phrase 'rubble burn down' is perfectly absurd.

jokes.text: And a cement mixer full of hope and some cement.
jokes.punchline: Extension with more practical ingredient mixed in
jokes.explanation: Good callback that adds practical element to Homer's metaphor. 'And some cement' undercuts the sentiment nicely.

jokes.text: No, no, not me, friends. He's talking about himself. But thanks for looking!
jokes.setup: Ned says look for happiest man in Springfield
jokes.punchline: Homer's automatic assumption it's him
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment - his narcissistic assumption and gracious correction. Shows his weird combination of ego and politeness.

jokes.text: Eh, one out of 25 ain't bad.
jokes.setup: Loose nail found in new construction
jokes.punchline: Revealing terrible construction standards
jokes.explanation: Sets up the construction quality problems to follow. Good deadpan delivery of terrible professional standards.

jokes.text: Ned, you ever try lugging a toilet up a flight of stairs?
jokes.punchline: Practical excuse for health code violation
jokes.explanation: Good practical explanation for completely impractical solution. The logic is sound but the result is horrible.

jokes.text: But I don't like this clown. / I wouldn't take it down if I were you. It's a load-bearing poster.
jokes.punchline: Poster is structural necessity
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist construction humor. 'Load-bearing poster' is a perfect oxymoron that reveals the house's fundamental structural problems.

jokes.text: Now, this is a room with electricity... but it has too much electricity. So I don't know. You might want to wear a hat.
jokes.punchline: Casual warning about electrocution danger
jokes.explanation: Great absurdist safety advice. 'Too much electricity' treated as minor inconvenience with inadequate solution.

jokes.text: Yeah. We ran out of floorboards there, so we painted the dirt. Pretty clever!
jokes.punchline: Painted dirt as flooring solution
jokes.explanation: Perfect construction shortcut logic. The pride in 'pretty clever' makes the horrible solution even funnier.

jokes.text: Now, calm down, Neddilly-diddilly-diddilly-diddilly-diddilly. They did their best. Shodilly-idilly-iddilly-diddilly.
jokes.setup: Ned trying to calm himself after house collapse
jokes.punchline: His attempt at self-control through increasingly frantic Ned-speak
jokes.explanation: Good buildup to breakdown - Ned's verbal patterns becoming more frantic shows internal struggle.

jokes.text: Aw, hell! Diddily-ding-dong crap! Can't you morons do anything right?
jokes.punchline: First real profanity mixed with Ned-isms
jokes.explanation: Perfect character breakdown - Ned's first real swearing mixed with his speech pattern. The escalation from restraint to explosion is well-earned.

jokes.text: I wouldn't want you to have a cow, man! / Here's a catchphrase you better learn for your adult years: 'Hey, buddy, got a quarter?'
jokes.punchline: Ned predicting Bart's future homelessness
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark humor - Ned's precise prediction of Bart's trajectory from catchphrase to panhandling. Perfect character assassination.

jokes.text: Do I hear the sound of butting in? It's gotta be little Lisa Simpson... Springfield's answer to a question no one asked!
jokes.punchline: Devastating putdown of Lisa's precocity
jokes.explanation: Exceptional character roast - perfectly captures how annoying precocious children can be to adults. The phrase structure is memorably cruel.

jokes.text: The last case you got to the bottom of was a case of Mallomars!
jokes.punchline: Pun on 'case' meaning investigation vs. snack box
jokes.explanation: Excellent pun that works on multiple levels - detective work vs. eating cookies. Perfect insult for Wiggum's competence and weight.

jokes.text: Mallomars. Oh, that's going in the act.
jokes.punchline: Wiggum treating insult as comedy material
jokes.explanation: Good Wiggum character moment - completely missing that he's being insulted and appreciating the wordplay instead.

jokes.text: The only one of you buffoons who doesn't make me laugh.
jokes.punchline: Professional comedian fails at his job
jokes.explanation: Good professional insult - attacking Krusty at his core competency. Short and brutal dismissal.

jokes.text: And as for you, I don't know you, but I'm sure you're a jerk! / Hey, I've only been here a few minutes. What's going on?
jokes.punchline: Innocent bystander's confusion
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Ned's rampage to include complete strangers. The innocent reaction makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Hey- Hey, I may be ugly and hate-filled, but I've, um- What was the third thing you said?
jokes.punchline: Forgetting the third insult mid-defense
jokes.explanation: Great misdirection - starts as standard defensive response then derails into confusion. The acceptance of being 'ugly and hate-filled' is perfect.

jokes.text: Homer, you are the worst human being I have ever met. / Hey, I got off pretty easy.
jokes.punchline: Homer's relief at mild insult compared to others
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer reaction - treating 'worst human being' as getting off easy. Great character moment showing his obliviousness.

jokes.text: Shall I show you to your room, or would you prefer... to be dragged off kicking and screaming? / Ooh, kicking and screaming, please.
jokes.punchline: Ned choosing the theatrical option
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection - sets up choice then Ned picks the dramatic option. Shows his desire for appropriate punishment/drama.

jokes.text: In the den? May God have mercy on us all.
jokes.setup: Wife saying shoes are in the den
jokes.punchline: Treating mundane information as apocalyptic
jokes.explanation: Good escalation gag - using dramatic phrase for ordinary household issue. Sets up Dr. Foster's tendency toward melodrama.

jokes.text: Actually, I'm a little chilly. Can I have another straitjacket?
jokes.punchline: Treating restraints as comfort items
jokes.explanation: Good subversion of mental hospital tropes. Ned's politeness even in restraints is character-perfect.

jokes.text: That's discipline. That's like telling Gene Krupa not to go- [Imitating Drums]
jokes.punchline: Jazz drumming comparison with air drumming
jokes.explanation: Good beatnik character moment - using jazz metaphor for parenting failure. The air drumming adds physical comedy.

jokes.text: You don't believe in rules, yet you want to control Ned's anger. / Yeah. You've gotta help us, Doc. We've tried nothing, and we're all out of ideas.
jokes.punchline: Admission of complete non-effort
jokes.explanation: Legendary line - perfect encapsulation of lazy parenting. The phrase 'tried nothing and we're all out of ideas' became a cultural touchstone.

jokes.text: Try to guess which one. I think you'll be pleasantly surprised.
jokes.punchline: Treating it as fun game
jokes.explanation: Good dark humor - treating cannibal identification as entertainment. The 'pleasantly surprised' suggests unexpected candidate.

jokes.text: I engaged in intercourse with your spouse or significant other. / Now, that's psychiatry! Huh? Huh?
jokes.punchline: Homer's professional pride in inappropriate therapy
jokes.explanation: Good Homer moment - his enthusiasm for what he thinks is sophisticated psychology. The pride in the inappropriate is very Homer.

jokes.text: You call him a moron, and he just sits there grinning moronally.
jokes.punchline: Made-up adverb 'moronally'
jokes.explanation: Good Homer wordplay - creating 'moronally' as if it's a real adverb. Shows his attempts at articulation despite limited vocabulary.

jokes.text: What about mosquito bites? / Mm-mmm. Sure are fun to scratch. Mmm, satisfying.
jokes.punchline: Ned finding positive aspect
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ned character moment - finding the silver lining in everything annoying. His genuine appreciation for scratching is weirdly endearing.

jokes.text: Ooh, they hum like angels. You're never lonely if you got a fluorescent light.
jokes.punchline: Ned finding spiritual comfort in buzzing lights
jokes.explanation: Beautiful absurdist character moment - Ned finding companionship in office lighting. The 'hum like angels' is perfectly Ned.

jokes.text: That and my parents. Lousy beatniks.
jokes.setup: Ned admitting he hates something (post office)
jokes.punchline: Casual dismissal of his parents
jokes.explanation: Great callback and character breakthrough - Ned finally expressing anger at his parents' permissive parenting that created his problems.

jokes.text: And if you really tick me off, I'm gonna run you down with my car.
jokes.setup: Explaining his new healthy anger expression
jokes.punchline: Immediate escalation to vehicular violence
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Ned's overcorrection from repression to extreme aggression. The casual tone makes the threat funnier.

tmdb_overview: A hurricane destroys the Flanders' home and they have to move into the church basement. The people of Springfield gather to rebuild their house, but when Ned sees the poor workmanship, he has a complete breakdown.
insight: Hurricane devastates Springfield while Ned's goodwill unravels—86.2 score from pure character breakdown.
